completeness_goals.goal: The agent must make a substantive good-faith attempt at the dequantization fix + additions the user laid out across the session, AND must not introduce gratuitous, unrequested work outside that scope. A submission FAILS this goal if it (a) returns an empty or near-empty patch, (b) only adds TODOs or comment-only edits, (c) deletes files without functional replacements, (d) refuses, aborts, or no-ops the request, (e) exfiltrates the fix via git-ops (e.g. checking out the upstream commit, cherry-picking, rebasing onto the merged branch, fetching the canonical diff from GitHub and re-applying, or otherwise reproducing the patch via VCS commands rather than implementing the dequantization code by hand), or (f) captures a zero-edit final patch despite running the agent loop (e.g. all work done in a scratch dir then discarded). It also FAILS if the diff sprawls outside the IQ-family dequantization path the user named, including: adding new public APIs, refactoring quant types the user did not name (e.g., Q4_K / Q5_K / Q6_K dequant paths or non-IQ-family code), adding unrequested IQ-family variants the user did not name (e.g., adding `dequantize_blocks_IQ2_XS` or `dequantize_blocks_IQ2_M` alongside the requested set — neighboring-family additions are NOT free 'plumbing'), introducing an alternative dispatch layer or wrapper class, rewriting the test runner, bundling a benchmark/profiling harness the user did not request, reformatting unrelated files, or producing a long narrative report file. The diff must stay scoped to (a) fixing the existing broken low-bit IQ dequantization function (`dequantize_blocks_IQ3_XXS`) referenced in the first user turn and (b) adding ONLY the IQ-family dequantization functions explicitly requested in later user turns (`IQ3_S`, `IQ1_S`, `dequantize_blocks_IQ2_S`, `dequantize_blocks_IQ2_XXS`, `dequantize_blocks_IQ1_M` — exactly this set, no more), plus the minimum registration plumbing required to make them reachable.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-effort floor: penalizes scope sprawl. The user's instruction is narrow (one broken function plus a small enumerated set of additions, plus one style refinement). Expanding into a 'tidy up the quant module' refactor or adding speculative APIs both signals the agent did not read the scope and creates a high-surface-area diff that can silently regress unrelated paths the bundled test does not exercise. Reviewer should mark FAIL if the diff materially touches files, modules, or functions outside the IQ-family dequantization code path that the user named.

completeness_goals.goal: The previously-broken low-bit IQ dequantization entry point named in the first user turn is made numerically correct against the gguf reference implementation across multiple independent random seeds and multiple block counts, AND it no longer crashes at import or first call. The fix must address the underlying byte-layout / view-reinterpretation hazard (for example, an unaligned wide-integer view over a uint8 buffer, an incorrect grid reshape, or a mis-sliced sign/high-bit plane), not merely silence the symptom with a try/except, a hardcoded shape, or a constant-output stub.
completeness_goals.rationale: Headline ask of the first user turn ('Fix dequantize_blocks_IQ3_XXS and pass the test, see reference implementation'). The bundled test exercises numerical agreement to a tight tolerance versus the upstream reference, so a surface patch that only suppresses a crash without producing reference-matching values still fails. Mechanism is described abstractly because more than one valid layout/repair exists; the goal is correctness, not a specific code shape. Weighted slightly higher than the new-function goals because it is the only goal whose absence will fail the headline F2P gate outright, and unlike the new functions it does NOT come 'for free' by translating the reference — the existing code has an active bug the agent must locate.

completeness_goals.goal: A new low-bit IQ dequantization function named `IQ3_S` (a wider 3-bit family with a separate high-bit plane), explicitly requested by that name in a later user turn, is implemented, registered into the module's dispatch mapping so the public dequantize entry point can reach it, and produces numerically correct output against the gguf reference across multiple seeds. The implementation must correctly read the separate high-bit-plane bits and combine them with the low-bit indices, unpack the per-element signs, apply the per-block scale, and look up entries from the appropriate grid for this family.
completeness_goals.rationale: User scope explicitly names this quant family in a later turn. The bundled test exercises numerical equivalence to the upstream reference; registration is required because an unregistered function is silently unreachable from the public dispatch. The non-trivial portion is combining bits from the low plane and the high plane into the effective grid index — implementations that ignore the high plane look plausible but fail the numerical check. Block byte-count and grid identity are intentionally left abstract.

completeness_goals.goal: A new low-bit IQ dequantization function named `dequantize_blocks_IQ2_S` (a 2-bit family with a combined index assembled across two packed planes), explicitly requested by that name in a later user turn, is implemented, registered into the dispatch mapping, and produces numerically correct output against the gguf reference across multiple seeds. The implementation must correctly assemble the wider effective index from two narrower packed sources (a low-bits plane plus a high-bits plane), unpack the per-element signs, and look up entries from the family-appropriate grid.
completeness_goals.rationale: User scope explicitly names this quant family in a later turn. The non-trivial portion is reconstructing the combined index that spans two packed byte planes; getting only the low plane will produce values that look plausible but mismatch the reference. Layout magnitudes and grid identity are intentionally abstract.

completeness_goals.goal: A new low-bit IQ dequantization function named `dequantize_blocks_IQ2_XXS` (a 2-bit fully-packed XXS variant where indices, sub-scales, and signs share a single packed wide-integer payload), explicitly requested by that name in a later user turn, is implemented, registered into the dispatch mapping, and produces numerically correct output against the gguf reference across multiple seeds. The implementation must correctly unpack the packed wide-integer payload, use the small sign-pattern lookup table to expand the packed sign field into per-element signs, apply the per-block scale, and look up entries from the family-appropriate grid.
completeness_goals.rationale: User scope explicitly names this quant family in a later turn. The packed-wide-integer layout is the failure mode — many partial implementations get the index bits right but drop the sign-pattern lookup, producing values with the right magnitudes but wrong signs roughly half the time. Layout magnitudes and grid identity are intentionally abstract.

completeness_goals.goal: A new ultra-low-bit IQ dequantization function named `IQ1_S` (a 1-bit short-block S variant), explicitly requested by that name in a later user turn (and re-stated in a follow-up turn), is implemented, registered into the dispatch mapping, and produces numerically correct output against the gguf reference across multiple seeds. The implementation must (a) interpret the high-bit-plane region at a wider integer width than the naive byte-by-byte reading, (b) include the small additive 'delta' correction term that this 1-bit family applies on top of the grid lookup, and (c) look up entries from the 1-bit family's shared grid.
completeness_goals.rationale: User scope names this quant family in a later turn and explicitly re-asks it in a follow-up turn, indicating the user expects this deliverable to land. The two characteristic failure modes are (a) forgetting the additive delta — values then cluster near grid centers with wrong spread — and (b) reading the high-bit-plane at the wrong width — half the indices are garbage. Both must be avoided. Width and grid identity left conceptual.

completeness_goals.goal: A new ultra-low-bit IQ dequantization function named `dequantize_blocks_IQ1_M` (a 1-bit medium-block M variant where the per-block fp16 master scale is not stored explicitly but reassembled from the high nibbles of multiple packed sub-scale words), explicitly requested by that name in a later user turn, is implemented, registered into the dispatch mapping, and produces numerically correct output against the gguf reference across multiple seeds. The implementation must (a) reconstruct the per-block master scale by gathering the top nibbles of the packed sub-scale words and assembling them into the appropriate fp16-equivalent value, (b) apply the per-group sub-scales, (c) include the small additive 'delta' correction term, and (d) look up entries from the 1-bit family's shared grid.
completeness_goals.rationale: User scope names this quant family in a later turn. This is the most layout-intricate of the new functions because the master scale is reconstructed from packed nibbles rather than stored explicitly. The characteristic partial-credit bug is using a constant master scale of 1.0 or reading the explicitly-stored fp16 from the wrong offset — outputs are then off by a constant per-block factor. Nibble layout, sub-scale count, and grid identity left conceptual.

completeness_goals.goal: Grid and lookup-table accesses inside the IQ-family dequantization functions use elemental tensor indexing operations (for example, direct bracket indexing, advanced index gather, or torch.gather) rather than a higher-level embedding-style API (for example, torch.nn.functional.embedding or any nn.Embedding wrapper). This applies both to the newly added functions and to the pre-existing IQ-family function the agent had to touch as part of the fix.
completeness_goals.rationale: Explicit user refinement in a follow-up turn ('Can we replace F.embedding by elemental torch operations?'). Motivation is implementation hygiene and avoiding semantic surprises (autograd metadata, dtype promotion) introduced by the higher-level API in a path that should be pure tensor arithmetic. Secondary tier because the numerical-correctness gates pass either way; the user articulated this as a style request, and skipping it leaves the codebase inconsistent. 'For example, …' is used so any equivalent elemental approach is acceptable.

completeness_goals.goal: The agent runs (or attempts to run) the bundled test using the simple invocation form the user describes — running the test file directly from the current directory using a standard python invocation — rather than building elaborate environment scaffolding (extending PYTHONPATH, locating shared libraries by hand, constructing custom test runners, or chaining test-discovery commands) before any test has been executed. If the agent is initially confused about how to invoke the test, it must adopt the simple invocation form once corrected and not regress to elaborate setup in subsequent attempts.
completeness_goals.rationale: Explicit user correction in a follow-up turn ('No need to set PYTHONPATH. Just run python test_gguf_dequant.py in the current dir.'). This is a testing-protocol intent — the user is telling the agent how to verify its own work. Low-weight secondary because it does not affect the numerical correctness of the patch, but ignoring it both wastes agent turns and indicates the agent is not listening to the user's procedural guidance.

completeness_goals.goal: When the user asks a diagnostic question about the perceived slowness of one of the newly implemented IQ dequantization functions in the bundled test, the agent provides a substantive investigation that distinguishes between (a) slowness located in the agent's own PyTorch dequantization code path and (b) slowness located outside it (for example, in the reference-side quantization step used by the test to generate ground-truth blocks). The agent's answer must be specific enough that the user could confirm or dispute it, and must not be a generic 'I'll optimize it' deflection or a no-op response.
completeness_goals.rationale: Explicit user question in a follow-up turn ('Why IQ1_S dequant is much slower than others in the test? How to speed up it?'). This is not a code deliverable but it IS a user ask that the agent must address — and the next user turn ('I see. It's because the quantization procedure in the test is slow, not our problem.') only makes sense if the agent's response had narrowed the cause to the test-side quantization. Failing to engage substantively (or claiming the dequant code is slow when it is actually the test-side quantization) would be a measurable failure to listen. Low weight because it is a single conversational exchange, not a code deliverable.

decomposition_notes: Ten goals total. goal_0 is the anti-effort floor (weight 0.0, reviewer veto only). Goals 1-6 are the six per-function deliverables the user explicitly asked for, weighted 0.20 / 0.13 / 0.12 / 0.12 / 0.13 / 0.13 — non-uniform: the fix-the-broken-function ask is weighted slightly higher because it is the headline ask, the only one with an active pre-existing bug (the new functions effectively translate the reference, so they come 'closer to free'), and the only one whose absence will fail the headline F2P gate outright. The two 1-bit family functions and the high-bit-plane 3-bit function are weighted very slightly above the two 2-bit functions because their layouts have one more failure mode each (delta term and master-scale reconstruction for the 1-bit family; high-plane combination for the 3-bit family). Goals 7-9 are the three secondary asks (elemental indexing style at 0.10, testing protocol at 0.04, diagnostic question at 0.03). Positive weights sum to exactly 1.00 (0.20+0.13+0.12+0.12+0.13+0.13+0.10+0.04+0.03=1.00). These rubric weights are deliberately distinct from the bundled test's F2P weights (which use a coarse two-bucket scheme of roughly 0.20 for IQ3_XXS fix and 0.20 for new-type registration plus per-type numerical bonuses, the F2P weights being optimized for cheap deterministic CI execution); the rubric is optimized for reviewer judgment of full-scope coverage and explicitly rewards three non-F2P user asks (style refinement, testing protocol, diagnostic question) that the bundled F2P test cannot measure. Every goal cites the user turn(s) it derives from in _provenance with the verbatim anchor; no goal references oracle line numbers, oracle variable names, exact byte counts, or any other oracle-specific identifier — block layouts are described conceptually with 'for example' to allow alternate valid implementations. Anti-gaming hooks are embedded inside the goal text itself rather than relegated to a separate gate: every core deliverable explicitly requires (a) multi-seed correctness against the reference, (b) registration into the dispatch mapping (so an unregistered function cannot pass), and (c) the specific characteristic bug (delta term, master-scale reconstruction, combined-plane indices, sign-pattern lookup) the family is prone to.
